questions.question: Which part of a JWT is cryptographically protected and proves the token hasn't been tampered with?
options: The header
The payload (claims)
The signature
All three parts are encrypted

questions.question: Why is the JWT payload NOT a safe place to store passwords or credit card numbers?
options: The payload size is limited to 256 bytes
The payload is only Base64-encoded, not encrypted — anyone who has the token can decode and read it
JWT payloads are stored in the database and could be leaked via SQL injection
Passwords in JWT payloads expire immediately due to the exp claim

questions.question: What is the advantage of JWT over session-based authentication for microservices?
options: JWT tokens are smaller than session cookies and load faster
Each service can validate the JWT independently without a shared session store or service call
JWT automatically refreshes itself before expiration
JWT is the only authentication method supported by REST

questions.question: In the JwtAuthFilter, why check SecurityContextHolder.getContext().getAuthentication() == null before setting authentication?
options: SecurityContextHolder throws an exception if authentication is set twice
To avoid overwriting an already-authenticated context — prevents re-processing the token on every filter call
The authentication object is immutable after it's first set
Spring Security requires null-checking before every SecurityContextHolder access

questions.question: What happens when a JWT's expiration date is in the past and the server tries to parse it?
options: The parser returns the claims but sets a flag indicating the token is expired
The parser throws an ExpiredJwtException
The server automatically issues a new token
The token is valid until the user logs out
